perturbation.original_text: The confidential, proprietary and trade secret information of the disclosing party (“Confidential Information”) to be disclosed hereunder is (i) information in tangible form that bears a “confidential,” “proprietary,” “secret,” or similar legend, and (ii) discussions relating to that information whether those discussions occur prior to, concurrent with, or following disclosure of the information.
perturbation.changed_text: The confidential and proprietary information of the disclosing party (“Protected Data”) to be disclosed hereunder is (i) information in tangible form that bears a “confidential,” “proprietary,” “secret,” or similar legend, and (ii) discussions relating to that information whether those discussions occur prior to, concurrent with, or following disclosure of the information.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "Confidential Information" to "Protected Data", while the rest of the contract refers to "Confidential Information", creates ambiguity. The change of the name of the variable it will create ambiguity on later sections, where the contract will refer to "Confidential Information", but in the section that was changed, it refers to "Protected Data" instead. This contradiction can be found on the section where it refers to 'Obligations of Receiving Party', where "Confidential Information of the disclosing party" is mentioned.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Obligations of Receiving Party. The receiving party will maintain the confidentiality of the Confidential Information of the disclosing party with at least the same degree of care that it uses to protect its own confidential and proprietary information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care under the circumstances. The receiving party will not disclose any of the disclosing party’s Confidential Information to any employees or to any third parties except to the receiving party’s employees, parent company, affiliates and subsidiaries who have a need to know and who agree to abide by nondisclosure terms at least as comprehensive as those set forth herein; provided that the receiving party will be liable for breach by any such entity. For the purposes of this Agreement, the term "employees" shall include independent contractors of each party. The receiving party will not make any copies of the Confidential Information received from the disclosing party except as necessary for its employees, parent company, affiliates and subsidiaries with a need to know. Any copies, which are made, will be identified as belonging to the disclosing party and marked "confidential", "proprietary" or with a similar legend.
perturbation.justification: The original text defined 'Confidential Information'. The changed text replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Protected Data'. The 'Obligations of Receiving Party' section refers specifically to 'Confidential Information'. This creates a direct contradiction because the receiving party's obligations are now unclear: are they responsible for 'Confidential Information' as defined elsewhere, or only 'Protected Data' as defined in the modified section? The change breaks the contract's internal consistency regarding what information is protected, leading to potential disputes. Therefore, a strong in-text contradiction exists as per the Misaligned Terminology definition.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party will maintain the confidentiality of the Confidential Information of the disclosing party with at least the same degree of care that it uses to protect its own confidential and proprietary information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care under the circumstances.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party will maintain the security of the Confidential Information of the disclosing party with at least the same degree of care that it uses to protect its own confidential and proprietary information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care under the circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'confidentiality' to 'security' implies a difference in the obligation. 'Confidentiality' refers to keeping the information secret, while 'security' refers to protecting the information from unauthorized access, which can be interpreted as a weaker obligation. This contradiction arises because the agreement continues to discuss "confidentiality obligations" in later sections, making it unclear whether the receiving party's primary duty is to maintain secrecy or simply ensure data protection, since in section 3 refers to 'Termination of Obligation of Confidentiality'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Termination of Obligation of Confidentiality. The confidentiality obligations set forth in this Agreement shall bind the Parties for a period of five (5) years from the date of disclosure of Confidential Information, unless any of the exceptions set forth in Section 4 below occurs.
perturbation.justification: The original text refers to 'confidentiality obligations', which is then changed to 'security obligations'. The contradicted text refers to 'Termination of Obligation of Confidentiality'. The change from 'confidentiality' to 'security' creates a direct contradiction because the contract now discusses security when the contradicted text refers to confidentiality, causing uncertainty about which obligation is in effect as per the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party will not disclose any of the disclosing party’s Confidential Information to any employees or to any third parties except to the receiving party’s employees, parent company, affiliates and subsidiaries who have a need to know and who agree to abide by nondisclosure terms at least as comprehensive as those set forth herein; provided that the receiving party will be liable for breach by any such entity.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party will not disclose any of the disclosing party’s Confidential Information to any staff or to any third parties except to the receiving party’s employees, parent company, affiliates and subsidiaries who have a need to know and who agree to abide by nondisclosure terms at least as comprehensive as those set forth herein; provided that the receiving party will be liable for breach by any such entity.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "employees" to "staff", the contract introduces potential ambiguity regarding the scope of authorized recipients of the confidential information. Since the contract does define employees as 'independent contractors of each party' it is not clear wether this definition also applies for staff. The agreement mentions 'employees' in the paragraph after the definition, generating a contradiction since, if the defition of 'employees' does not apply, independent contractors are not included to the protection of confidential information, generating a loophole.
perturbation.contradicted_text: For the purposes of this Agreement, the term "employees" shall include independent contractors of each party.
perturbation.justification: The change from "employees" to "staff" creates a contradiction because the contract defines "employees" to include independent contractors. By using "staff", which is not explicitly defined, it introduces ambiguity as to whether independent contractors are included in the group of people who can receive confidential information. This contradicts the original definition of "employees" and its intended scope, potentially excluding independent contractors from the protection of confidential information as defined elsewhere in the contract where "employees" is used.

perturbation.original_text: The confidentiality obligations set forth in this Agreement shall bind the Parties for a period of five (5) years from the date of disclosure of Confidential Information, unless any of the exceptions set forth in Section 4 below occurs.
perturbation.changed_text: The confidentiality obligations set forth in this Agreement shall bind the Parties for the Term from the date of disclosure of Confidential Information, unless any of the exceptions set forth in Section 4 below occurs.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "five (5) years" to "the Term" without defining 'Term' creates a significant ambiguity. This directly contradicts the intention of specifying a duration for confidentiality, so now the obligation is inndefinite. The Term is not defined anywhere else, but it contradicts with the statement specifying 5 years, causing the contract to be ambiguous and unenforceable on that term. A direct contradiction happens with the original statetment where the duration of the confidentiality was set in 5 years.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The confidentiality obligations set forth in this Agreement shall bind the Parties for a period of five (5) years from the date of disclosure of Confidential Information, unless any of the exceptions set forth in Section 4 below occurs.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the duration of the confidentiality obligations from 'five (5) years' to 'the Term', which is undefined within the contract. This creates a contradiction because the contradicted text clearly specifies a duration of 'five (5) years'. This is a direct conflict, making the confidentiality term ambiguous and potentially unenforceable. The explanation clearly states that by removing the explicit timeframe and replacing it with an undefined term, the contract now suffers from misaligned terminology, causing an in-text contradiction as per the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' perturbation definition.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party will not be liable for the disclosure of any Confidential Information which is: (a) generally made available publicly or to third parties by the disclosing party without restriction on disclosure; (b) rightfully received from a third party without any obligation of confidentiality; (c) rightfully known to the receiving party without any limitation on disclosure prior to its receipt from the disclosing party; (d) independently developed by employees and / or independent contactors of the receiving party; or (e) required to be disclosed in accordance with applicable laws, regulations, court, judicial or other government order, provided that the receiving party shall give the disclosing party reasonable notice prior to such disclosure and shall comply with any applicable protective order.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party will not be liable for the disclosure of any Confidential Information which is: (a) generally made available publicly by the disclosing party without restriction on disclosure; (b) rightfully received from a third party without any obligation of confidentiality; (c) rightfully known to the receiving party without any limitation on disclosure prior to its receipt from the disclosing party; (d) independently developed by employees and / or independent contactors of the receiving party; or (e) required to be disclosed in accordance with applicable laws, regulations, court, judicial or other government order, provided that the receiving party shall give the disclosing party reasonable notice prior to such disclosure and shall comply with any applicable protective order.
perturbation.explanation: By removing "or to third parties" from exception (a), the contract now creates an inconsistency. Before, the receiving party was not liable if the disclosing party made the information available to third parties without restriction. Now, the receiving party *is* potentially liable if the information, while publicly available, was initially disclosed *to* a third party by the disclosing party. This introduces confusion about the scope of the exception, in special when the confidentiality obligations of the receiving party is mentioned at the termination section 3.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Termination of Obligation of Confidentiality. The confidentiality obligations set forth in this Agreement shall bind the Parties for a period of five (5) years from the date of disclosure of Confidential Information, unless any of the exceptions set forth in Section 4 below occurs.
perturbation.justification: The change removes "or to third parties" from the exception to confidentiality. Originally, the receiving party wasn't liable if the disclosing party made the information available to third parties. Now, the receiving party *is* potentially liable if the information, while publicly available, was initially disclosed *to* a third party by the disclosing party. This directly impacts the 'Termination of Obligation of Confidentiality' clause, because the 'exceptions set forth in Section 4' no longer fully protect the receiving party if the disclosure was initially to a third party, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the sole and entire agreement between the parties with respect to the Confidential Information and all restrictions thereon; it supersedes any and all prior or contemporaneous oral or written agreements, negotiations, communications, understandings and terms, whether express or implied regarding the Confidential Information, and may not be amended except in a writing signed by a duly authorized representative of the respective parties. Any other agreements between the parties, including non-disclosure agreements, will not be affected by this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement constitutes the sole and entire agreement between the parties with respect to the Confidential Data and all restrictions thereon; it supersedes any and all prior or contemporaneous oral or written agreements, negotiations, communications, understandings and terms, whether express or implied regarding the Confidential Information, and may not be amended except in a writing signed by a duly authorized representative of the respective parties. Any other agreements between the parties, including non-disclosure agreements, will not be affected by this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "Confidential Information" to "Confidential Data" in the first part of the sentence creates ambiguity, as the text later reverts to using the term "Confidential Information". This contradiction is present because the second part of the sentence refers to the "Confidential Information" but the Agreement initially refers to "Confidential Data".
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement constitutes the sole and entire agreement between the parties with respect to the Confidential Data and all restrictions thereon; it supersedes any and all prior or contemporaneous oral or written agreements, negotiations, communications, understandings and terms, whether express or implied regarding the Confidential Information, and may not be amended except in a writing signed by a duly authorized representative of the respective parties. Any other agreements between the parties, including non-disclosure agreements, will not be affected by this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text refers to 'Confidential Information' consistently. The changed text introduces 'Confidential Data' in the initial part of the sentence while reverting to 'Confidential Information' later in the same sentence. The contradicted text is the same as the changed text, meaning that the very same sentence now uses two different terms ('Confidential Data' and 'Confidential Information') which creates an ambiguity and a contradiction, since it's unclear if they are meant to be the same thing or not. This aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction', as the contract uses multiple terms interchangeably without defining them.
